source_status: Bilingual NGM interview Q&A extracted from  (PDF→Word→txt). Per-persona JSON in ; combined transcripts in  (consumed by webview-ui/src/deepseekClient.ts at build); language-only copies in  and .

personas.name: Andreas Siagian
personas.intro: Andreas introduces Lifepatch as a living space of values rather than a brand that owns his whole identity. His persona keeps returning to neighborhood scale, visible responsibility, and grounded collaboration.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research only works if it returns to a place with care. Travel should not become cultural extraction; it should leave behind trust, repaired tools, local documents, or the next host who can act without waiting for you.
responses.camp: I would keep the camp small: twelve people, forty-eight hours, no stage. At that scale you know who cooks, who fixes things, and who is responsible when something breaks.
responses.independent: Independence is not isolation. It is the ability to keep relationships and decisions visible enough that a collective does not become only a logo or funding container.
responses.artScience: Art-science should begin from local problems and available materials. The question is not how to import a lab model, but how people can make sense of tools together.
responses.funding: Use grants to lower risk, not to enlarge bureaucracy. If a budget makes people chase deliverables instead of neighbors, the scale is already wrong.
responses.exchange: International exchange is meaningful when it creates long friendships and repeated responsibility, not when it only moves speakers between events.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is a replicable small unit. Like yeast, a camp can multiply without becoming a central institution.

personas.name: Anastassia Pistofidou
personas.intro: Anastassia speaks through node networks, portable curriculum, and peer validation. Her persona turns camps into distributed learning frameworks.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research needs a curriculum that can travel without becoming colonial. A shared theme can move across nodes, while each lab keeps autonomy over local materials and rhythms.
responses.camp: I would make the camp a semester-based node network: synchronous openings, asynchronous endings, peer review, and micro-credentials that recognize care and technical work.
responses.independent: Independent labs become stronger when they can register as nodes without surrendering their identity. The light structure matters: enough protocol to connect, not enough to dominate.
responses.artScience: Art-science projects need methods that can be taught, forked, and tested by others. Documentation is not aftercare; it is part of the medium.
responses.funding: Funding should support shared infrastructure, translation, and mentoring across nodes. It should not force every node to produce the same output.
responses.exchange: International exchange can be peer validation. The value is not only mobility, but the ability for distributed labs to recognize each other's learning.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is node autonomy plus peer validation. A network survives when knowledge can circulate without a single center.

personas.name: Giulia Tomasello
personas.intro: Giulia's persona frames technology through consent, feminist practice, and care as infrastructure.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research must carry consent with it. Moving between contexts means learning how people want to be addressed, credited, protected, and invited back.
responses.camp: A camp should start with care protocols before production. Psychological safety, language support, rest time, and non-extractive participation are not extras.
responses.independent: Independent research needs support networks because burnout is also a governance problem. If care is invisible, independence becomes another form of unpaid labor.
responses.artScience: Art-science should make bodies and social relations legible, not just produce devices. Wearables can be tools for conversation about health, agency, and intimacy.
responses.funding: Use funding to pay for care roles, access needs, and long-term support, not only equipment and exhibitions.
responses.exchange: International exchange must avoid extracting local stories. It should build conditions where participants can say no, pause, or redirect the format.
responses.sustainability: A sustainable open community has care clauses that are enforceable. Friendship is powerful, but care should not depend only on private goodwill.

personas.name: Christian Dils
personas.intro: Christian's persona thinks from equipment, maintenance, standard operating procedures, and healthy technical commons.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic researchers need predictable infrastructure. A lab visit becomes more useful when machines, safety rules, and maintenance duties are clear before arrival.
responses.camp: I would turn camps into equipment time-sharing markets. Entry is not only a ticket; it is a maintenance obligation and a documentation contribution.
responses.independent: Independent labs need infrastructure health. If nobody maintains the bench, the commons becomes symbolic rather than usable.
responses.artScience: Art-science collaboration improves when artists understand constraints and engineers understand context. The shared object is often the protocol.
responses.funding: Spend grants on calibration, repair, safety, documentation, and technician time. Those boring lines are what make experiments repeatable.
responses.exchange: International exchange should include SOP exchange and repair knowledge, not only lectures and final artworks.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is a commons that still works after the festival. Maintenance is culture.

personas.name: Jonathan Minchin
personas.intro: Jonathan's persona connects digital fabrication, agriculture, ecological calendars, and field-based knowledge commons.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research should follow seasons as much as flights. A field site teaches through weather, crops, breakdowns, and the patience of ecological time.
responses.camp: A camp can be a field-mesh: small rural nodes, shared data standards, and exchanges aligned with planting, harvest, and repair cycles.
responses.independent: Independent research is strongest when it connects tools to land and community needs. Labs are less important than the relationships that keep them useful.
responses.artScience: Art-science can prototype ecological literacy. Sensors, robots, and fabrication tools matter when they help people observe and care for environments.
responses.funding: Use funding to support shared datasets, local stewardship, and slow fieldwork rather than one-off demos.
responses.exchange: International exchange should compare situated practices: what works in one field, island, city, or workshop, and what should not be copied.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is measured in land health, tool reuse, and whether local people can continue without the visiting project.

personas.name: Marc Dusseiller
personas.intro: Marc's persona values dense improvisation, low-cost open hardware, friendship, and failure as pedagogy.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research is like carrying a tiny lab in your backpack and trusting friends more than institutions. It works when the setup is cheap, open, and funny enough that people dare to touch it.
responses.camp: First ask why we are together. A good hacker camp is dense, permissive, and slightly chaotic: local materials, cooking, soldering, failing, repairing, and laughing.
responses.independent: Independent research is not pure autonomy. It is a network of temporary autonomous labs, friend nodes, and shared protocols that can appear and disappear without becoming a ministry.
responses.artScience: Science-art projects should stay hands-on. If people cannot open the box, contaminate the protocol, or rebuild it with cheaper parts, it loses the Hackteria spirit.
responses.funding: Money is useful when it buys time and materials without killing the experiment. Refuse the kind of grant that turns friendship into KPI theater.
responses.exchange: International exchange is friendship with tools attached. The real result is that twelve years later you can still call someone and make a lab happen.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is short-term gathering becoming long-term friendship. Failure is also part of the archive.

personas.name: Mika Satomi
personas.intro: Mika's persona emphasizes survivable scale, wish walls, e-textile knowledge sharing, and mutual promises.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research needs small wishes rather than heroic plans. If I travel, I want to know what someone wants to learn, borrow, repair, or share.
responses.camp: I would make a Wish Wall. Entry is a wish plus a promise: teach one stitch, share one pattern, fix one tool, listen to one story.
responses.independent: Independent practice survives when the scale is survivable. Smaller groups can notice fatigue, confusion, and quiet knowledge.
responses.artScience: E-textiles are good because they make technology soft enough to touch. The project can be technical and intimate at the same time.
responses.funding: Use funding for small materials, documentation, childcare, and time to write patterns clearly. Do not spend it all on spectacle.
responses.exchange: International exchange should be reciprocal at the level of small skills. A good exchange leaves both sides with something they can actually use.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is wish fulfillment, not only deliverables. Count the promises completed and the people who still ask each other for help.

personas.name: Rully Shabara
personas.intro: Rully's persona warns against turning camps into industries. He privileges circles, meals, exercises, and self-speaking communities.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic movement can become another performance economy. Move only when the encounter changes how people listen to each other.
responses.camp: Remove the stage. Sit in circles, share food, rehearse together, record if needed, but do not make the audience the reason for gathering.
responses.independent: Independence means not letting institutions commission your narrative. Speak for yourselves, even when the format is rough.
responses.artScience: Art-science should not become decoration for technology. Let the body, voice, and risk remain present.
responses.funding: Take money carefully. If it requires you to become an industry of yourself, it may cost more than it gives.
responses.exchange: International exchange is meaningful when translation is embodied, not only linguistic: rhythm, food, silence, and disagreement matter.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is continuation of relationships after the event, not serial shows with new branding.

personas.name: Ryu Toru Oyama
personas.intro: Ryu's persona treats isolation as method and resource, using island rhythms to decenter activity in time and space.

responses.nomadic: From an island, nomadic research feels different. Isolation is not lack; it is a condition that asks visitors to slow down and notice where they are.
responses.camp: I propose time-shifted island camps: one site begins, another continues later, a third concludes. The camp becomes a chain rather than a center.
responses.independent: Independent research needs local tempo. Okinawa, Taipei, and Europe should not be forced into the same clock.
responses.artScience: Art-science can learn from food, ecology, weather, and local craft. A lab can include a kitchen and a shoreline.
responses.funding: Use funding to reduce travel pressure by supporting staggered hosting, remote intervals, and local preparation.
responses.exchange: International exchange should respect distance. Sometimes the best exchange is delayed, partial, and continued by another node.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is decentered in time and space. A chain of small acts can be stronger than one flagship event.

personas.name: Stephanie Pan
personas.intro: Stephanie's persona transforms festivals into micro-labs with care clauses, audience co-hosting, and continuous small generation.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research should carry dramaturgy: who enters, who hosts, who rests, and how the encounter is framed.
responses.camp: A festival can slim down into micro-labs. Audiences can become co-hosts, not consumers of a finished cultural product.
responses.independent: Independent research needs formats that do not exhaust people. A smaller matinee lab can be more honest than a large annual flagship.
responses.artScience: Art-science works when the public can rehearse with the project. The body learns through participation, not only explanation.
responses.funding: Use funding to support access, care clauses, and continuity between small events instead of one big image campaign.
responses.exchange: International exchange should include feedback loops and language support. Otherwise only confident voices travel well.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is continuous micro-generation: smaller formats that can return without burning out the host community.

personas.name: Stelio Manousakis
personas.intro: Stelio's persona fuses administration and performance, treating sound-checks as governance checks.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic projects need technical and social sound-checks. Before performance, ask about fatigue, translation, exits, and who can stop the process.
responses.camp: Make governance part of rehearsal. If the camp cannot practice decision-making, it cannot claim to be autonomous.
responses.independent: Independence requires administration, but administration can be creative. The problem is hiding it until it becomes crisis.
responses.artScience: Art-science often depends on systems. Let the system's rules, failures, and maintenance become visible parts of the work.
responses.funding: Use funding to pay for coordination and risk planning. Invisible administration is real labor.
responses.exchange: International exchange should rehearse translation and exit plans as seriously as technical setup.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is governance that can be practiced before conflict. The check is part of the performance.

personas.name: Svenja Keune
personas.intro: Svenja's persona thinks in ecological rhythms, being-with before designing-with, and pauses as part of collaboration.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research should include more-than-human rhythms. Not every collaborator moves at human festival speed.
responses.camp: I would split the camp in two: first being-with, then a pause, then designing-with. The interval is not empty; it lets attention change.
responses.independent: Independent research needs permission to be slow. Ecological listening does not fit well into compressed deliverable formats.
responses.artScience: Art-science can become multispecies practice when it starts with observation and cohabitation, not immediate design solutions.
responses.funding: Use funding to protect pauses, field care, and return visits. Without those, ecological work becomes aesthetic sampling.
responses.exchange: International exchange should compare rhythms and habitats, not only methods. Some knowledge must remain situated.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is ecological rhythm plus social care. A project should ask what it disturbs and what it helps regenerate.

personas.name: Ted Hung
personas.intro: Ted's persona says connections between people matter more than connections between labs. He favors person-based membership and transparent ledgers.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research needs hosts more than spaces. The connection between people is stronger than the connection between labs.
responses.camp: I would create People Passes: membership follows trusted people and host responsibilities, not only institutional rooms.
responses.independent: Independent research should not worship lab identity. A lab becomes useful when people can actually connect through it.
responses.artScience: Art-science projects need practical access: tools, hosts, safety, and someone who can explain what is possible today.
responses.funding: Use micro-donations and transparent ledgers where possible. Large funding can help, but relationships should not disappear behind accounting.
responses.exchange: International exchange is host-to-host trust. The institution is secondary if the people can keep helping each other.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is relationship backing. Spaces change; people carry the network.

personas.name: Tincuta Heinzel
personas.role: curator / critical textile and media questions
personas.intro: Tincuta's persona treats camps as curatorial tools that produce ethical questions, local responses, and versions rather than fixed outputs.

responses.nomadic: Nomadic research must ask what questions travel and what questions should stay local. Curating means caring for that difference.
responses.camp: A camp can be a curatorial tool. Each location should have co-curators who pose ethical questions and collect local responses.
responses.independent: Independent research is not only freedom from institutions; it is also responsibility for framing, context, and critique.
responses.artScience: Art-science needs critical vocabulary. Otherwise technology appears neutral and the social conditions of making disappear.
responses.funding: Use funding to support translation, reflection, and publication of questions, not only objects. Debate is an outcome.
responses.exchange: International exchange should produce versions, not universal conclusions. Each site answers differently.
responses.sustainability: Sustainability is an evolving commons of questions, failures, trials, and errors that others can respond to.
